coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence of potassium permanganate and carbon dots and its analytical applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

coredata.dc:description: Propyl Gallate (PG) is widely utilized as an antioxidant in edible oils. However, prolonged consumption of excessive PG may result in toxic effects on the kidneys, skin, and respiratory system, thereby posing significant health risks. In this study, boron-doped carbon dots (B/CDs) were synthesized using Astragalus membranaceus and boric acid as precursor materials. The B/CDs were characterized by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). These results indicated that B/CDs possess excellent dispersibility, a surface abundant in functional groups, and remarkable optical properties. It was found that B/CDs significantly enhanced the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of the acidic KMnO4 system, even in the absence of added sensitizers. Based on these findings, a rapid and highly sensitive PG CL sensor was developed. PG exhibited a linear response over the concentration range of 1 × 10−7 mol/L to 6 × 10−4 mol/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 8.7 × 10−9 mol/L and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.1 % (n = 11). The developed CL system demonstrated excellent selectivity and anti-interference capabilities. This method was successfully applied for the detection of PG in sesame oil, pepper oil, and rapeseed oil, with recovery rates ranging from 95.3 % to 104.3 %.

Carbon Dots
Chemiluminescence
Propyl Gallate
Edible Oils

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. V: Injection Valve, PMT: photomultiplier tube.

(a) TEM image of B/CDs, (b) Raman spectrum of B/CDs, (c) FT-IR spectrum of B/CDs, (d) XPS survey scan spectrum of B/CDs, (e) B 1 s spectrum of B/CDs, (f) O 1 s spectrum of B/CDs, (g) C 1 s spectrum of B/CDs, (h) UV–vis absorption of B/CDs, insert: images of the B/CDs under daylight (right) and 365 nm UV light (left), (i) FL spectrum of B/CDs.

(a) CL curve of HCl+KMnO4, HCl+KMnO4+B/CDs, HCl+KMnO4+AM+BA, AM: Astragalus membranaceus, BA: boric acid (b) CL intensity of B/CDs with different synthesis days, (c) CL intensity of B/CDs under different pH conditions.

(a) selective study, the concentration of PG was 1 × 10−5 mol/L, the concentrations of the other compounds were 5 × 10−5 mol/L, (b) synthesis temperature of B/CDs (160–220℃, 5 h), (c) synthesis time of B/CDs (2–8 h, 190℃), (d) B/CDs dilution ratio, (e) concentration of HCl, (f) concentration of KMnO4.

(a) Linear relationship between CL intensity and PG concentration, (b) CL spectrum of a: HCl+KMnO4, b: HCl+KMnO4+B/CDs, c: HCl+KMnO4+B/CDs +PG, (c) Effect of different free radical scavengers on the CL intensity.
Detection limits and linear ranges of different methods for PG detection.
Effect of common interfering substances on the determination of PG.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Concentration ratio refers to the ratio between interfering substances and PG.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence of potassium permanganate and carbon dots and its analytical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Propyl Gallate (PG) is widely utilized as an antioxidant in edible oils. However, prolonged consumption of excessive PG may result in toxic effects on the kidneys, skin, and respiratory system, thereby posing significant health risks. In this study, boron-doped carbon dots (B/CDs) were synthesized using Astragalus membranaceus and boric acid as precursor materials. The B/CDs were characterized by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). These results indicated that B/CDs possess excellent dispersibility, a surface abundant in functional groups, and remarkable optical properties. It was found that B/CDs significantly enhanced the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of the acidic KMnO4 system, even in the absence of added sensitizers. Based on these findings, a rapid and highly sensitive PG CL sensor was developed. PG exhibited a linear response over the concentration range of 1 × 10−7 mol/L to 6 × 10−4 mol/L, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 8.7 × 10−9 mol/L and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.1 % (n = 11). The developed CL system demonstrated excellent selectivity and anti-interference capabilities. This method was successfully applied for the detection of PG in sesame oil, pepper oil, and rapeseed oil, with recovery rates ranging from 95.3 % to 104.3 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Propyl Gallate (PG) is extensively utilized as a food antioxidant to prevent the oxidation and rancidity of fats, thereby preserving the flavor, color, and nutritional value of oils. PG finds broad applications in fried foods, canned goods, meats, and oils, and is approved as a synthetic food antioxidant by both the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) (Javaheri-Ghezeldizaj et al., 2023). However, research indicates that prolonged consumption of excessive PG may induce toxic effects on the kidneys, skin, and respiratory system, and can lead to mitochondrial dysfunction, thereby posing significant health risks. As a result, many countries have implemented regulatory limits on the use of PG in foods. For instance, China's National Food Safety Standard for the Use of Food Additives (GB2760–2011) permits its use in edible oils, fried foods, biscuits, instant noodles, and canned nuts, with a maximum limit of 0.1 g/kg (Dai et al., 2016). Currently, the primary methods for PG detection include capillary electrophoresis coupled with, electrochemistry (Ye et al., 2022), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Abad-Gil et al., 2022). Although these methods provide accurate detection of PG, they are often complex, time-intensive, and necessitate costly instrumentation. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop detection methods that are rapid, convenient, and cost-effective for PG analysis.

Chemiluminescence (CL) is a phenomenon that arises from the emission of electromagnetic radiation by electronically excited intermediates produced during chemical reactions. These intermediates can either directly emit light or transfer energy to another molecule that serves as the emitter, resulting in indirect luminescence (Li et al., 2011). Owing to its high sensitivity, wide detection range, rapid analysis speed, and relatively simple instrumentation, CL has been extensively applied across diverse fields (Zhao et al., 2023). Of the various CL reagents, KMnO4 functions as both a source of luminescent species and an oxidant, playing a pivotal role in the CL detection of numerous organic compounds (Rahman, 2020). Under acidic conditions, KMnO4 predominantly emits red light. Numerous studies have amplified the CL intensity of acidic KMnO4 systems by employing various sensitizers, including polyphosphates, formaldehyde, sulfides, and fluorescent compounds (Adcock et al., 2014). Recently, with the rapid advancements in nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) and other nanomaterials have been increasingly employed to enhance CL intensity and expand the analytical capabilities of diverse CL systems (Sun et al., 2020). Compared with the study of Su et al. (2016), the present study synthesized CDs using biomass raw materials, which is more environmentally friendly and reduces the potential threat to operators by eliminating the toxic reagent of HCHO.

Carbon Dots (CDs) are a novel nanomaterial, typically with a particle size of less than 10 nm. They possess a surface rich in functional groups and a core composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon structures containing oxygen and nitrogen groups (Mukherjee et al., 2022). CDs exhibit exceptional optical properties, can be synthesized from abundant materials, and are produced using straightforward methods (Zhu et al., 2015). Additionally, they exhibit high water solubility and biocompatibility, rendering them highly suitable for applications in bioimaging (Wang et al., 2014), photocatalysis, sensing and detection (Szczepankowska et al., 2023), and drug delivery (Omer et al., 2022). The primary methods for synthesizing CDs include electrochemical oxidation(Rocco et al., 2023), microwave (Liu et al., 2024), and solvothermal methods (Tian et al., 2016). However, these methods typically require complex instrumentation and involve cumbersome procedures. In contrast, the hydrothermal method offers a more straightforward approach, synthesizing CDs in a single step under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, thereby eliminating the need for additional post-processing and enabling rapid CDs production. Recently, the use of natural biomass materials as precursors in CDs synthesis has garnered increasing attention from researchers. (Kanwal et al., 2022) Several studies have reported the development of simple synthesis strategies that yield CDs with distinctive properties, which have been subsequently employed in the design and fabrication of various sensor systems (Shen et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022).

In this study, boron-doped carbon dots (B/CDs) were synthesized using the natural Chinese medicinal herb Astragalus membranaceus, as the carbon source and boric acid as the dopant via a one-step hydrothermal method. B/CDs are characterized by high quantum yield, excellent optical properties, good water solubility and good dispersion. Remarkably, the B/CDs significantly enhanced the CL intensity of an acidic KMnO4 system without the need for any additional sensitizers, resulting in an enhancement factor of approximately 62-fold. Furthermore, the addition of propyl gallate (PG) led to a further increase in the CL intensity of the B/CDs-sensitized system. Based on this enhancement phenomenon, a CL sensor for the detection of PG was developed, and the mechanism of B/CDs-enhanced CL was discussed. The designed method was successfully applied to the determination of PG in real edible oil samples.

TEM images were recorded using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope (FEI, USA). FTIR measurements were performed using a VERTEX 70 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). CL measurements were conducted using an MPI-B multi-parameter chemiluminescence analysis system (Xi'an Remex Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd.). Flow injection was performed with an IFIS-D intelligent flow injection sampler (Xi'an Remex Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd.). Fluorescence (FL) and CL spectra were obtained using an FS5C fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Tianmei Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectra of B/CDs were recorded using a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer (UK). UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a TU-1810DSPC UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd.).

Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Ascorbic acid (AA), β-carotene and thiourea (TU) were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co. Ltd., KMnO4, and PG were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Three edible oil varieties (rapeseed, pepper, and sesame oil) were sourced from a local supermarket. Stock solutions of 2 × 10−3 mol/L KMnO4, 2 mol/L HCl, and 1 × 10−3 mol/L PG were prepared by dissolving KMnO4 solid, HCl solution, and PG solid in deionized water, respectively. All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and all of them used deionized water. The information of other reagents is shown in Table S1.

The synthesis process for B/CDs is depicted in Fig. 1. B/CDs were synthesized using waste Astragalus membranaceus and boric acid as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method. A total of 1.5 g of Astragalus membranaceus and 0.5 g of boric acid were weighed into a 100 mL beaker, followed by the addition of 50 mL deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to achieve full dispersion. After sonication, the mixture was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated in an electric oven at 190°C for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the autoclave was removed and allowed to cool to room temperature. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was filtered multiple times through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble substances. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (Mw = 500–1000) for 24 h. The final product, referred to as B/CDs, was stored at 4°C for future use.

The flow injection analysis configuration is illustrated in Fig. 2. Two peristaltic pumps, each operating at a speed of 30 revolutions per minute, are used to introduce all system solutions into a spiral glass flow cell. Initially, KMnO4, HCl, and B/CDs are first mixed through two three-way valves. Next, PG and the carrier H2O are combined with the other solutions through an additional three-way valve, and the mixture flows into the spiral glass flow cell, where CL is generated. The CL signal is detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT), with the high voltage set to 700 V and an amplification factor of 4. The computer converts the light signal into a digital signal via dedicated software. The concentration of PG is determined by calculating the change in the CL signal, with the relative CL intensity represented as ΔI, where ΔI = I0 - I1, and I0 and I1 correspond to the CL intensity with and without PG, respectively. Statistical analysis of actual sample spiking recoveries was performed using the student's t-test, and all data processing as well as plotting was performed using Origin 2021.

Actual sample handling is based on literature (Wang et al., 2017), Measure 2 mL of edible oil and dissolve it in 20 mL of petroleum ether. After sonication for 10 min, the mixture was transferred to a separatory funnel, and 20 mL of 16.7 g/L ammonium acetate solution was added, followed by 2 min of shaking. The mixture was allowed to stand until phase separation occurred. The aqueous layer was then transferred to a new separatory funnel. If emulsification occurred, the emulsion layer was also removed. The petroleum ether layer was extracted twice with 20 mL of 16.7 g/L ammonium acetate solution, followed by two washes with 15 mL of triple-distilled water each time. The aqueous layers were combined and allowed to stand. The solution was then filtered through filter paper and transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask. The filter paper was rinsed with a small volume of triple-distilled water, and the volumetric flask was filled to the mark with triple-distilled water and shaken well. A portion of the solution from the volumetric flask was filtered through filter paper, with the first 20 mL of filtrate discarded. The remaining filtrate was preserved for further analysis.

Fig. 3.a presents the TEM image of B/CDs, providing a clear view of their morphology and distribution. The B/CDs display a regular quasi-spherical structure, with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 6 nm, and an average size of 2.69 nm (Zhao et al., 2023). The inset in the top-right corner of Fig. 3.a confirms the B/CDs crystal structure with a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm, which is consistent with the graphene (100) planes(Han et al., 2020). Fig. 3.b shows the Raman spectrum of B/CDs, with two characteristic peaks at 1340 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, representing the disordered D band (sp3 hybridization) and crystalline G band (sp2 hybridization), respectively, confirming the graphite structure of the B/CDs (Li et al., 2022). Compared with the Raman spectroscopic results of undoped CDs(Figure S1), B-element doping reduces the G peaks of CDs, giving larger surface defects and enhancing the CL performance of B/CDs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3.c) shows absorption peaks at 1455 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, and 903 cm−1, corresponding to the B-O and C-B (Feng et al., 2020) stretching vibrations. Additionally, a peak at 1132 cm−1 is attributed to the B-OH stretching vibration, signifying successful B doping (Wang et al., 2023). Peaks at 1263 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C and CC (Wibrianto et al., 2024), while those at 2929 cm−1 and 3222 cm−1 are assigned to the C−H and O−H bendi ng vibrations. Peaks at 1701 cm−1 and 1668 cm−1 are related to CO stretching vibrations. The elemental composition of B/CDs was further studied using XPS. Fig. 3.d illustrates the XPS survey spectrum of B/CDs, showing characteristic peaks at 532.2 eV, 284.8 eV, and 199.7 eV, corresponding to O 1 s, C 1 s, and B 1 s, respectively. In the high-resolution B 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3.e), two distinct peaks at 191.21 eV and 192.61 eV represent B-C and B-O, respectively (Zhang et al., 2024). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3.f) shows peaks at 531.69 eV and 532.02 eV, assigned to C–O–H and C–O–C. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3.g), peaks at 284.81 eV, 285.63 eV, and 286.12 eV correspond to C–B, C–C/CC, and C–OH, respectively. These spectra confirm that the XPS findings align with the FT-IR results, verifying successful B doping. This process effectively passivated and functionalized the carbon dots' surface, thereby improving the CL performance of B/CDs.

The UV absorption spectrum of B/CDs in Fig. 3.h reveals a pronounced absorption peak around 310 nm, associated with the n-π* transition of CO. The inset in Fig. 3.h demonstrates the bright white light emitted by B/CDs under 365 nm UV illumination. The FL spectrum of B/CDs, shown in Fig. 3.i, was measured at 10 nm excitation increments. At the maximum excitation wavelength of 500 nm, the strongest emission occurred at 570 nm. The emission intensity of B/CDs initially increases and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength, which is attributed to the varying surface states and surface bandgaps of B/CDs (Shamsipur et al., 2018). The fluorescence quantum yield of B/CDs was 8.32 %, calculated as shown in Eq.S1, using quinine sulfate as the reference.

The CL properties of B/CDs were studied using a flow injection system. As shown in Fig. 4.a, mixing KMnO4 with HCl produced a weak chemiluminescence emission. In contrast, when B/CDs were introduced into the KMnO4-H⁺ solution, the CL intensity surged by approximately 62-fold. To confirm whether the increased CL intensity originated from B/CDs, a solution containing the same concentration of raw materials (Astragalus membranaceus and boric acid) used in B/CD synthesis was tested in the system. The results demonstrated that neither the Astragalus membranaceus nor the boric acid solution enhanced the CL system, confirming that the significant increase in CL intensity was attributed to B/CDs.

To assess the stability of synthesized B/CDs, the CL intensity was monitored over different synthesis durations (1, 3, 7, 15, and 31 days) and across a range of pH conditions (pH 2–13). As depicted in Fig. 4.b, the CL intensity of B/CDs remained relatively consistent for synthesis periods up to 15 days, showing minimal variation. After 31 days, however, the CL intensity of B/CDs dropped by around 7.3 %. B/CDs exposed to various pH conditions maintained a significant sensitizing effect on the CL system (Fig. 4.c), although the enhancement was slightly lower in alkaline conditions compared to acidic ones. This difference is attributed to the acidic nature of the overall reaction environment. These findings suggest that B/CDs possess excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study and optimal detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In the selectivity study, various antioxidants and competitive metal ions commonly found in food were tested to observe their effects on the CL intensity of the designed sensor. These included Co2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ag2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, as well as antioxidants like gallic acid (GA), Butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA), Butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ), vitamin E, green tea polyphenols (GTP), superoxide dismutase (SOD), sulfites, and carotenoids (all at concentrations of 5 ×10−5 mol/L) along with 1 × 10−5 mol/L PG. As shown in Fig. 5.a, some antioxidants, including BHA, BHT, TBHQ, and SOD, demonstrated slight inhibitory effects on the CL intensity at higher concentrations. However, when the concentration was reduced, their impact on the system became negligible. In contrast, low concentrations of PG significantly enhanced the CL intensity, indicating that the designed CL sensor has good selectivity for PG.

Fig. 5.b-f depict the optimization of the system’s solution concentrations to determine the optimal conditions for the CL sensor in PG detection. Initially, the effect of B/CDs synthesized under various conditions on CL intensity was examined, revealing that B/CDs heated at 190°C for 5 h produced the highest CL intensity. Considering the concentration of the synthesized B/CDs as 1, the effects of the concentrations of B/CDs, KMnO4, and HCl on the CL intensity were investigated. The results suggested that the optimal conditions for PG detection include B/CDs at a 1:60 ratio, 1 mol/L HCl, and 0.7 × 10−3 mol/L KMnO4.

PG at varying concentrations was analyzed under optimal experimental conditions (Figure. S2). As shown in Fig. 6.a, a good linear relationship was observed between PG concentration and CL intensity, ranging from 1 × 10−7 mol/L to 6 × 10−4 mol/L. The regression equation is ΔI = 43.162x+ 42.314, with an R² = 0.9991, where x represents the concentration of PG (10−6 mol/L). The detection limit is 8.7 × 10−9 mol/L (S/N = 3, LOD=3 *SD/K). Intra-day precision was assessed by measuring PG at a concentration of 6 × 10−6 mol/L across 11 parallel determinations, resulting in a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.1 %. Inter-day precision was evaluated by analyzing PG at the same concentration over three days, yielding an RSD between 2 % and 2.7 %. These findings demonstrate that the developed CL sensor possesses high sensitivity and precision. Compared with other methods, our proposed method shows a wider or comparable linear range, as well as a lower detection limit than most of the methods previously used for the detection of PG, as shown in Table 1. This method expands the application of synthetic CDs from biomass raw materials in chemiluminescent systems as well as in food analysis.

The luminescent properties of the acidic KMnO4 system have been previously reported, with Mn (II) being the primary luminescent species in the system (Adcock et al., 2014). The CL spectrum typically shows a distinct peak around 600 nm. The impact of adding other substances with catalytic and fluorescent properties on the luminescent species remains uncertain. To investigate the effect of B/CDs on the luminescent species in the system, the CL spectrum was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 6.b, curve a, the KMnO4 system without B/CDs exhibits a prominent peak at 630 nm, consistent with previous reports. With the addition of B/CDs, the peak shifts to 510 nm, corresponding to the fluorescence emission of B/CDs. Additionally, the peak associated with Mn (II) disappears, indicating the formation of a new luminescent species, B/CDs* (Fig. 6.b, curve c).

To confirm the formation of B/CDs* , free radical scavenging experiments were performed using AA, TU, NBT, and β-carotene. Ascorbic acid (AA) is a potent antioxidant capable of scavenging •O2 – through reductive reactions (Xiao et al., 2024), NaN3 is an effective quencher for 1O2 through physical quenching, TU reacts with hydroxyl radicals and is a powerful scavenger of •OH (Whiteman, Halliwell, 1997), nitroblue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) can react with •O2 – to form blue mono-methylhydrazine (Yamakoshi et al., 2003), β-carotene is a carotenoid with the ability to scavenge different types of free radicals, including •OH and •O2 – (Yildiz et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 6.c, all four scavengers inhibited the system's CL intensity, indicating the involvement of •O2 –, •OH, and 1O2 in the luminescence process. Due to the generation of O2 from KMnO4 and HCl, a reactive oxygen chain reaction is initiated in water to produce 1O2 (Adcock et al., 2014). Paired 1O2 can form an excited state (1O2)2* (H. Liu et al., 2018), which returns to its ground state by releasing energy, producing CL signal. The emission wavelength of (1O2)2* is not visible in the CL spectrum. This absence is attributed to the unique optical properties of B/CDs, which overlap with the absorption spectrum of (1O2)2* , leading to chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) (Amjadi et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2020; Zong et al., 2018). Consequently, (1O2)2* transfers its energy to B/CDs, forming the excited state B/CDs* . The inhibition of CL intensity by β-carotene further supports the role of 1O2 in CL emission.

Amjadi et al. reported that CDs can be directly oxidized by strong oxidants to form holes (Amjadi et al., 2014). As a strong oxidant, KMnO4 can inject holes into B/CDs, forming B/CDs•+, •OH can also react with B/CDs to generate B/CDs•+ (Teng et al., 2014). The surface of B/CDs contains reductive groups (-OH, -COOH), which confer strong reducing abilities, •O2 – provides electrons to reduce B/CDs, forming B/CDs•–. Subsequently, electron transfer annihilation between B/CDs•+ and B/CDs•– generates the excited state B/CDs* , which then returns to the ground state, releasing energy and producing CL. The effective scavenging of •O2 – and •OH by AA, NBT, and TU reduces the formation of B/CDs* and consequently decreases the CL intensity, which is consistent with the free radical scavenging experiment results. Based on these findings, the proposed CL mechanism for the B/CDs-KMnO4 system is illustrated in Scheme 1.

Since the ions and compounds present in the actual sample detection procedure would appear to interfere with the results. The anti-interference ability of the KMnO4-B/CDs system was evaluated by examining the effect of co-existing ions on the CL intensity during the detection of 5 × 10−6 mol/LPG, and the tolerance was defined as the amount of co-existing substances that would not produce a ± 5 % error in the determination of PG, and the results are shown in Table 2. It was found that 1000 times the amount of Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2–, SO3 2–, HCO3 –, Al3+, NO3 –, NH4 +, HCOO–, Cl–, 500 times HSO3 –, Ca2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, BrO3 –, Glucose, K+, 100 times Cu2+, CO32-, Starch, C2O4 2–, BO3 3–, Fe3+, 50 times Fe2+, Hg2+, I–, Cu2+ coexisting interfering ions did not interfere with the detection of PG. These results indicate that the method demonstrates strong anti-interference ability and is suitable for detecting PG in real samples.

The precision and accuracy of the developed CL sensor were evaluated by measuring PG levels in three commercially available edible oils, following the extraction procedure outlined in Section 2.5, the results are shown in Table 3. A Student's t-test (P = 0.05) revealed no significant difference between the spiked and measured values. The accuracy of the method was evaluated by assessing PG recovery in spiked samples using the standard addition method. Recovery rates for the three oil samples ranged from 95.3 % to 104.3 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 1.5 % and 5 % (n = 5). These results confirm the method’s accuracy and its feasibility for analyzing real samples.

In this study, waste traditional Chinese medicine Astragalus membranaceus was used as a precursor to synthesize B/CDs through a one-step hydrothermal process. The experimental results indicate that B/CDs significantly enhance the CL intensity of the acidic KMnO4 system, which contains no additional sensitizers. Building on this finding, a novel CL sensor was designed for the detection of PG. The sensor demonstrates high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, strong interference resistance, and good stability, offering a wide linear range and a low detection limit. CL spectroscopy and free radical scavenging experiments confirmed the luminescent species in the system to be B/CDs* . Furthermore, the method was successfully applied to detect PG in various edible oils, highlighting the sensor’s practical applicability. This study provides a feasible method for detecting PG levels in food samples.

Xian Yang: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration. Qinfang Shi: Validation, Software. Yuxian Mai: Methodology, Investigation. Mengyue Ma: Methodology, Investigation. Qian Ming: Methodology, Investigation. Ying Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaoqin Long: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qiuxiong Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yue Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yujie Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xue Zhang: Supervision, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.107156.
